title: Psalms 98
subtitle: Sing to the LORD a New Song, for He Has Done Marvellous Things

sections.header: Verses 1–3 — “His Right Hand Has Won the Victory”

heb.paragraph: "Sing to the LORD a new song, for he has done marvellous things; his right hand and his holy arm have worked salvation (yəshūʿâ) for him" (v.1). God’s salvation is PUBLIC: made known (v.2), revealed (v.2), remembered (v.3). All the ends of the earth have seen the salvation of our God (v.3). The victory is not hidden but displayed.

notes.note: "His right hand has worked salvation. He has revealed his righteousness to the nations. All the ends of the earth have seen the salvation of our God." The visibility of salvation: revealed, seen, proclaimed. Nothing secret. MacArthur emphasizes the doctrinal significance of this passage within the framework of systematic theology. The text establishes principles that remain normative for the church — not merely descriptive of ancient Israel but prescriptive for how God's people should understand His character and respond in obedience.

refs.note: Mary's Magnificat draws on the psalm's imagery of God's arm winning victory and remembering mercy.
The LORD baring his holy arm so all ends of earth see salvation echoes the psalm's language.
The saints singing the song of Moses and the Lamb after victory over the beast fulfills this psalm.

hist.context: Psalm 98, like Psalm 96, calls for a 'new song' celebrating YHWH's victory. The 'right hand and holy arm' (v.1) recall exodus deliverance. God's salvation has been 'made known' and 'revealed' to nations — the hidden is now public. This missionary scope influenced Isaac Watts's famous paraphrase 'Joy to the World.'

sections.header: Verses 4–9 — “Let the Rivers Clap Their Hands”

heb.paragraph: "Let the rivers clap their hands (māḥăʾū), let the mountains sing together for joy" (v.8). Shout, burst into song, make music with harp, trumpets, ram’s horn (vv.4–6). Then nature joins: the sea, the world, the rivers, the mountains — all celebrate before the LORD who comes to judge (v.9). "He will judge the world in righteousness and the peoples with equity" (v.9).

notes.note: "Shout for joy to the LORD, all the earth, burst into jubilant song with music. Make music with the harp, with trumpets and the blast of the ram’s horn." Full orchestra + full volume + full earth.

"Let the sea resound. Let the rivers clap their hands; let the mountains sing." Rivers clapping — the most exuberant personification in the Psalter. Nature is not a backdrop; it is a participant. The judge who comes brings equity.

refs.note: Every creature in heaven, earth, and sea praising God fulfills the psalm's cosmic worship.
Creation groaning and waiting for liberation anticipates the jubilation the psalm envisions.
Every knee bowing in heaven, on earth, and under earth develops the psalm's universal scope.

hist.context: The orchestra expands from human music (harp, trumpets, horn) to nature itself: sea, world, rivers, and mountains joining the chorus (vv.7-8). This personification of creation praising the coming Judge envisions cosmic harmony restored. The Judge comes 'to judge the earth with righteousness' — judgment is good news for creation.

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: All the ends of the earth have seen the salvation of our God…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVHe has remembered his love and his faithfulness to Israel; all the ends of the earth have seen the salvation of our God.ESVHe has remembered his steadfast love and faithfulness to the house of Israel. All the ends of the earth have seen the salvation of our God.NIVThe Lord has made his salvation known and revealed his righteousness to the nations.ESVThe LORD has made known his salvation; he has revealed his righteousness in the sight of the nations.

rec.text: ""His right hand has worked salvation. He has revealed his righteousness to the nations. All the ends of the earth have seen the salvation of our God." The visibility of salvation: revealed, seen, proc…"

rows.label: Victory Revealed — 98:1–3
rows.text: "His right hand has won the victory." Made known. All the ends of the earth have SEEN.

hebtext.note: "Sing to the LORD a new song, for he has done marvellous things; his right hand and his holy arm have worked salvation (yəshūʿâ) for him" (v.1). God’s salvation is PUBLIC: made known (v.2), revealed (v.2), remembered (v.3). All the ends of the earth have seen the salvation of our God (v.3). The victory is not hidden but displayed.

"Let the rivers clap their hands (māḥăʾū), let the mountains sing together for joy" (v.8). Shout, burst into song, make music with harp, trumpets, ram’s horn (vv.4–6). Then nature joins: the sea, the world, the rivers, the mountains — all celebrate before the LORD who comes to judge (v.9). "He will judge the world in righteousness and the peoples with equity" (v.9).

themes.note: Worship peaks at 10: rivers clapping, mountains singing. Full creation orchestra. Mission: all ends of the earth have seen. Sovereignty: God’s arm wins the victory.

prayer_prompt: God has done marvellous things; the rivers clap their hands. Consider asking God what marvellous things He's done that you've stopped marvelling at.
